query: proton beam therapy use in craniopharyngioma

retrieved_docs.title: Craniopharyngioma(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Radiotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation treatment is used in patients with residual disease or to prevent recurrences. Radiation therapy includes conventional external radiotherapy, proton beam therapy, stereotactic radiotherapy, radiosurgery, and brachytherapy. The goal of radiotherapy is to decrease tumor burden while protecting essential neural structures. Specific Gy doses have been designated for every radiation modality. Multiple reports have suggested decreased mortality with slightly reduced morbidity following radiation therapy. Despite this, radiation therapy has not been proven to reduce the recurrence rate. Therefore, it continues to be an adjuvant modality to neurosurgical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniopharyngioma -- Treatment / Management -- Radiotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation therapy includes conventional external radiotherapy, proton beam therapy, and radiosurgery. [42] Radiation aims to decrease tumor burden while protecting essential neural structures. Proton therapy may provide better cognitive outcomes than photon therapy, which has similar survival outcomes. [43] Multiple reports have suggested decreased mortality with slightly reduced morbidity following radiation therapy. Despite this, radiation therapy has not been proven to reduce the recurrence rate. Therefore, it continues to be an adjuvant modality to neurosurgical intervention. [44] [45] In cases of primarily cystic tumors, intracystic catheters can be introduced into the cyst, allowing for frequent aspiration of the cyst contents and reducing the size of the target for radiation. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Nerve Glioma -- Radiation Oncology -- Proton Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Proton therapy has garnered significant interest, especially in treating pediatric tumors. The unique physical properties of protons compared to x-ray photons, especially the manner of dose deposition in the form of a Bragg peak and the lack of an exit dose, may reduce the volume of normal tissue irradiated and, thus, late toxicity. Dosimetric studies comparing 3D conformal photons with protons showed a 47% decrease in optic nerve dose, an 11% decrease in chiasm dose, a 13% decrease in pituitary gland doses, and a 39% decrease in temporal lobe dose compared to photon plans. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Palliative Radiation Therapy for Brain Metastases -- Technique or Treatment -- Radiotherapy Fields and Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Concomitant exposure to vital structures needs to be reduced while treating benign tumors such as pituitary adenoma. An attempt may be made to target the site of interest while preserving target structures nearby, even at sites for which double exposure may be required. Virtual planning CT scanning should be done before irradiation for planning. Virtual CT simulation when part of the brain is being irradiated.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothalamic Hamartoma -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical
retrieved_docs.content: Gamma knife radiosurgery (GKR)

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Complications -- Radiation-induced Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Radiation-induced tumors can emerge as a result of cranial radiotherapy. It is mainly a concern in the pediatric population, but as cancer therapies improve, a growing cohort of patients may experience radiation-induced tumors. There was a recent large-scale retrospective cohort study of patients that received cranial radiotherapy for pituitary or craniopharyngiomas with over 20 years of follow-up. The patient received a median of 45 to 50.4Gy, similar to what would be prescribed to patients with low-grade gliomas. [64] The 20-year cumulative risk was 4% in radiated patients but 2.1% in non-radiated patients. [64] The vast majority of secondary tumors were benign (88%), with meningioma being the most common. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Radiation Oncology -- Delivery
retrieved_docs.content: Typically, two modalities can be utilized in this setting: Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT) and Proton therapy. IMRT is the most common technique allowing for highly conformal treatment plans that, while leading to a low dose bath of radiation, can generally spare critical structures sufficiently to reduce the risk of long-term side effects. Proton therapy can be used in special circumstances, especially in children, to further reduce the dose to critical structures and hypothetically reduce the risk of long-term toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniopharyngioma -- Treatment / Management -- Intracystic Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Brachytherapy is primarily used to treat purely cystic craniopharyngiomas. [47] [48] Intracystic therapy with radioactive isotopes, bleomycin, and interferon-alpha has been used to induce tumor fibrosis and sclerosis. [49] A disadvantage of this option is the risk of severe neurotoxicity due to cystic leakage of the sclerosing substance.

retrieved_docs.title: Ewing Sarcoma -- Radiation Oncology -- Proton Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding oncologic outcomes, the 3-year local control with proton therapy was 92% with radiotherapy alone and 100% when combined with surgery. [34] These outcomes were comparable to other series using photon-based treatments. [34] Patients with non-metastatic Ewing sarcoma of the skull base treated with proton therapy had a 4-year local control rate of 96%. [38] However, this was not without late toxicities, such as bilateral hearing loss and vasculopathy. [38] While using proton therapy in children has theoretical advantages compared to photons, stereotactic body radiotherapy or brachytherapy may also offer highly conformal treatments and spare normal tissues and protons in many cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniopharyngioma -- Treatment / Management -- Hormonal Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be monitored postoperatively by checking morning cortisol, as more than 50% of children can be diagnosed with adrenal insufficiency later after surgery or radiation treatment. Children are usually treated with prednisone or hydrocortisone. The glucocorticoid replacement dose is 6 to 9 mg/m 2 with dose adjustment with increasing age and weight. Vasopressin Deficiency

retrieved_docs.title: Craniopharyngioma(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment for craniopharyngiomas is challenging because of their location, invasiveness, and proximity to adjacent neurovascular structures. Multiple modalities can be implemented in the management of craniopharyngioma, including surgery, radiotherapy, and intracystic therapy. The choice of treatment, surgery type, and extent should be individualized for each patient. The patient's age, underlying medical co-morbidities, tumor location, type and invasiveness, and ultimately neurosurgeon experience should be considered while deciding the treatment strategy. There is no consensus on the best treatment regimen. [6] [7] [8] [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Hamartoma -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: High-dose radiations on a focused location, such as the hypothalamus hamartomatous lesion, can be effective. Gamma knife radiosurgery uses highly focused gamma rays; therefore, it is a distinctly precise procedure. [22] Although it uses highly focused radiation beams and is relatively safer than traditional radiation therapy, it can also cause various adverse effects such as nausea, vomiting, fatigue, skin blistering, dysphagia, brain edema, and headache. Long-term treatment with radiation also increases the risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Pituitary Cancer -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: There are varying modalities of diagnosis that typically involve a combination of laboratory testing and radiographic imaging. This will be determined by the physician on a case-by-case basis. Treatment typically includes surgical intervention followed by possibly adjuvant radiosurgery and/or chemotherapy/immunotherapies.

retrieved_docs.title: Craniopharyngioma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment for craniopharyngiomas remains challenging because of their location, invasiveness, and proximity to adjacent neurovascular structures. Multiple modalities can be implemented in managing craniopharyngioma, including surgery, radiotherapy, and intracystic therapy. The choice of treatment, surgical approach, and extent of resection should be individualized for each patient. The patient's age, underlying medical comorbidities, tumor location, type and invasiveness, and, ultimately, neurosurgeon experience should be considered while determining the optimal treatment strategy.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Radiation Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: External beam radiation is typically delivered as IMRT using conformal techniques to minimize exposure to nearby structures such as the optic nerves, optic chiasm, lens, retina, brain, brainstem, spinal cord, and salivary glands. Case series describe prescribed dose ranges of 30 to 50 Gy, administered in daily fractions of 1.8 to 2 Gy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 410
retrieved_docs.content: Nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures represent a broad category of imaging and therapeutic techniques that employ radioactive isotopes. Within this category, radioisotope studies of the central nervous system are a specific type of “examination using radioactive isotopes” (الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة) and are classified under other nuclear medicine imaging. These central‑nervous‑system studies have further sub‑procedures, such as radionuclide identification of eye tumor, indicating a child‑parent hierarchy. Radionuclide imaging actions function as methodological approaches for radionuclide localization of tumors, and they are also described as methods of other nuclear medicine imaging. The process of radionuclide localization of tumor is a more narrowly defined activity that falls within the broader field of therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine. Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine itself is situated under the umbrella of radiation therapy and, like transmission imaging and stereotactic radiation surgery (الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية), is also linked to the general concept of examinations using radioactive isotopes. Intrakavitary radiotherapy is noted to be closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, radiation therapy, reinforcing its placement within the same therapeutic domain. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and functional network in which nuclear‑medicine diagnostic and therapeutic procedures are organized from general radioactive‑isotope examinations down to specialized imaging actions, tumor‑localization methods, and specific radiation‑therapy techniques, all of which are interrelated and essential for comprehensive patient assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 187
retrieved_docs.content: Nuclear medicine diagnostic procedure (procedure) is situated within a hierarchical framework of nuclear‑medicine and radiation‑therapy concepts. At the broadest level, radiation therapy serves as the parent category for Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine, which in turn encompasses the more specific activity of radionuclide localization of tumor. Radionuclide localization of tumor functions as a method for radionuclide imaging – action, and this imaging action is itself a method of the broader class of other nuclear medicine imaging. Within the diagnostic branch, Nuclear medicine diagnostic procedure (procedure) is the parent of the Radioisotope study of central nervous system; the latter is classified under other nuclear medicine imaging and has a child procedure, radionuclide identification of eye tumor. The Radioisotope study of central nervous system therefore links central‑nervous‑system imaging to a more specialized eye‑tumor identification technique. Overall, the relationships illustrate how therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine, radionuclide localization of tumor, and various imaging actions are interrelated subdomains of radiation therapy, while diagnostic procedures such as the Radioisotope study of central nervous system and its child, radionuclide identification of eye tumor, are nested within the larger category of other nuclear medicine imaging. This integrated hierarchy helps clinicians understand the connections between specific diagnostic studies, imaging methods, and therapeutic modalities, supporting more informed patient‑care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 376
retrieved_docs.content: Nuclear medicine diagnostic procedures are situated within a hierarchical framework of nuclear imaging techniques. These procedures are considered a child of the broader concept of radionuclide localization of tumor, indicating that methods aimed at locating tumors with radionuclides encompass a wider parent category. The same diagnostic procedures are also linked by an inverse‑is‑a relationship to the radioisotope study of the central nervous system, suggesting that they can be viewed as a specialized form of central nervous system (CNS) radioisotope examinations. The radioisotope study of the central nervous system belongs to the larger group of other nuclear medicine imaging and has a child concept, radionuclide identification of eye tumor, demonstrating that eye‑tumor imaging is a sub‑type of CNS radioisotope studies. The Arabic term الفحص باستخدام النظائر المشعة (examination using radioactive isotopes) serves as a parent concept for both the radioisotope study of the central nervous system and therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine, uniting diagnostic and therapeutic nuclear‑medicine activities under a common umbrella. Therapeutic radiology and nuclear medicine is itself a narrower concept within the domain of radionuclide localization of tumor, reflecting that tumor‑localization techniques are employed for therapeutic planning. The action of radionuclide imaging is described as a method of both radionuclide localization of tumor and other nuclear medicine imaging, indicating that this imaging action underlies both specific tumor‑localization procedures and more general nuclear‑medicine imaging modalities. Finally, the Arabic term الجراحة الإشعاعية التجسيمية (stereotactic radiosurgery) is placed under the same parent examination using radioactive isotopes, emphasizing its role as a therapeutic application that relies on precise radionuclide‑based imaging.
